codeReview.summary: NetworkOps.fetch wires `git fetch --all --prune --progress` through Git.stream inside focus.queue.run. Progress stderr flows through ProgressParser → ProgressCenter; success emits a green toast, failure classifies via ErrorClassifier and surfaces a red sticky toast with captured stderr detail. CancellationError silently ends progress without a toast — correct UX for focus-swap / window-close. StderrAccumulator actor is the right choice for a @Sendable closure needing mutable collection state. FetchToolbarButton is a thin wrapper with local `isRunning` guard and an accessibility identifier. Branch-list/status-header refresh after fetch is correctly FSEvents-driven (git writes to .git/FETCH_HEAD → existing .onChange(of: focus.lastChangeAt) observers reload) — avoids adding a fourth observer. 4 ViewModel tests using real bare-upstream fixtures (fetchReportsSuccess, fetchReportsErrorOnMissingRemote, fetchDrivesProgress, fetchSerializesInQueue) all green. Command exactness verified: args exactly ['fetch', '--all', '--prune', '--progress'].

issues.description: Inside the @Sendable Git.stream loop, `await MainActor.run { self.progress.update(...) }` hops back to MainActor for each progress update. NetworkOps is itself @MainActor, so the hop is only because focus.queue.run runs its body in a detached Task. Correct, but worth a one-line comment explaining why the explicit MainActor.run is needed — future readers will wonder why a @MainActor type needs to re-hop.

Git.stream's .completed event carries the exit code and we throw GitError.processFailed on non-zero. The thrown error's `.stderr` payload duplicates the accumulator's content — the catch block at line 240 re-reads the accumulator (correct) and the throw's stderr field is never read. Consider dropping stderr from the throw (pass empty string or 0-code sentinel) to avoid the implication that the throw's payload is authoritative. Non-blocking.

classify(error:stderr:) prefers the ErrorClassifier output over the already-typed GitError.processFailed thrown above. This is the right default (classified typed errors have better errorDescription) but means the original exit code is discarded. If a future caller wants to branch on exit code vs stderr for some edge case, the info is gone. Current callers don't need it; document the intent in a one-liner.

ProgressParser.consume(line + '\n') re-adds a newline stripped by Git.stream. If Git.stream ever changes to pass '\n'-terminated lines, this will double-newline and the parser's '\r'-split logic would need to handle that. The coupling is implicit. A brief comment at the call site locking in the contract ('ProgressParser wants newline-terminated feed; Git.stream emits bare lines') prevents future breakage.

fetchDrivesProgress only asserts progress.active.isEmpty at the end. The inline comment acknowledges the race — we can't reliably sample mid-fetch without instrumenting. A fully-deterministic alternative is to inject a progress sink that records append/remove events (Observable → observation proxy) or expand ProgressCenter to expose a monotonic event count. Defer; current test is adequate.

sharedStateObservations.observation: focus.queue.run wraps the entire stream lifetime, not just the launch — matches INTERFACES.md guidance and GraphModel.performReload precedent. A user-triggered fetch + auto-fetch tick + a future pull/push all serialise correctly via CompletionGate chain.

M5-fetch does NOT add a 4th per-focus observer. The FSEvents-driven refresh (branch list + status header reloads after fetch writes to .git/) leverages the existing 3 observers (branch/status/graph). Good discipline — AGENTS.md pre-M7 consolidation guidance stands.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: handoff M5-fetch.json whatWasImplemented: 'Branch-list/status-header refresh after fetch is FSEvents-driven (VAL-BRANCH-006)'
